skill.name: Pseudotime Trajectory Visualization
skill.description: Analyze single-cell RNA-seq data to infer developmental trajectories, compute pseudotime values, and generate publication-ready visualizations of cellular differentiation processes.
skill.summary: Automated pseudotime trajectory inference and visualization for single-cell RNA-seq data using diffusion maps and PAGA methods.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Security review dismissed all 119 static analysis findings as false positives. The skill runs Python scripts to perform legitimate single-cell trajectory analysis using standard bioinformatics libraries (scanpy, scvelo, palantir). All flagged 'shell backtick executions' are markdown code blocks containing example commands in documentation. 'Path traversal' flags are from a security checklist documenting prevention measures. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'certificate/key' flags are from argparse descriptions and Python dictionary operations. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, command injection vectors, or credential access detected.

low_findings.title: External Command Execution in Documentation (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 92 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in README.md, SKILL.md, and references/runtime_checklist.md. All instances are GitHub-flavored markdown code fences (triple backticks) containing example shell commands for user reference. No actual shell execution occurs from documentation. These are standard usage examples for a command-line tool.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequences in Security Checklist (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged '../' strings at SKILL.md:310 and SKILL.md:313. These lines are within a security checklist documenting actions to PREVENT path traversal: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. The scanner incorrectly flagged security documentation as the vulnerability itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 6 instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' across scripts/main.py and SKILL.md. Lines in scripts/main.py contain argparse description text and parameter help strings. Lines in SKILL.md contain markdown table headers and section descriptions. No cryptographic operations exist anywhere in the skill code or documentation.

low_findings.title: Certificate/Key File Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged scripts/main.py:136 for 'certificate/key files'. Line 136 is a Python dictionary membership check: `if embedding_key not in adata.obsm.keys():`. This checks for data embedding keys in an AnnData object, not cryptographic certificate or key files.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 5 instances of 'system reconnaissance' in pseudotime-trajectory-viz_audit_result_v2.json and scripts/main.py. The JSON file is the scanner's own audit output which naturally references system information. The Python file contains a print statement for missing genes, not reconnaissance activity.

low_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination Alerts (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged CRITICAL 'DANGEROUS COMBINATION: Code execution + Network + Credential access' and HIGH 'SUSPICIOUS COMBINATION: Filesystem + Credentials + Network'. These heuristics incorrectly combined: (1) legitimate Python script execution for data analysis, (2) a GitHub repository URL in documentation, and (3) no actual credential access. No credential access, data exfiltration, or network calls exist in the code.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write Operations (Legitimate)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged file write operations in scripts/main.py at lines 406, 544, 561. These are legitimate operations: creating output directories with os.makedirs and writing analysis results (JSON reports, CSV data) to user-specified output paths. This is expected and necessary behavior for a data analysis tool.

content.user_title: Generate Pseudotime Trajectory Visualizations for Single-Cell RNA-Seq Data
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to interpret complex cellular differentiation pathways from single-cell RNA-seq data. This skill automates trajectory inference and pseudotime analysis to produce publication-ready visualizations of developmental processes.
actual_capabilities: Compute diffusion pseudotime (DPT) trajectories from AnnData single-cell data using scanpy
Generate publication-ready trajectory plots with lineage assignments and pseudotime distributions
Visualize gene expression dynamics along pseudotime with scatter plots and heatmaps
Automatically detect root cells using stemness marker expression analysis
Support multiple trajectory methods including diffusion maps and PAGA graph abstraction
Export analysis results as CSV data files, JSON reports, and high-resolution figures
limitations: Requires preprocessed AnnData (.h5ad) files with normalized and log-transformed data
Assumes cellular differentiation is the main source of biological variation in the data
Memory usage may exceed 16 GB for datasets with over 50,000 cells
Circular or cyclic biological processes are not well represented by linear pseudotime models

use_cases.title: Analyze Stem Cell Differentiation Trajectories
use_cases.description: Map the differentiation path of stem cells into mature cell types using diffusion pseudotime analysis on single-cell RNA-seq data.
use_cases.target_user: Developmental biologist studying cell fate decisions and lineage commitment

use_cases.title: Identify Lineage-Specific Gene Markers
use_cases.description: Track expression patterns of marker genes across pseudotime to identify genes that drive specific lineage branches during differentiation.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researcher investigating gene regulation in development

use_cases.title: Visualize Disease Progression Pathways
use_cases.description: Apply trajectory analysis to understand how cells transition from healthy to diseased states, revealing intermediate cell populations and branch points.
use_cases.target_user: Translational researcher studying cellular mechanisms of disease progression

prompt_templates.title: Basic Trajectory Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run pseudotime trajectory analysis on my single-cell data using the file at path [DATA_PATH]. Use diffusion pseudotime as the method and save output to [OUTPUT_DIR].
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick start for users who want a default trajectory analysis pipeline

prompt_templates.title: Gene Expression Along Trajectories
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the trajectory in [DATA_PATH] and plot expression of genes [GENE1,GENE2,GENE3] along pseudotime. Use diffusion method and generate both individual trend plots and a combined heatmap.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user investigating specific gene dynamics during differentiation

prompt_templates.title: Custom Lineage Branching Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run pseudotime analysis on [DATA_PATH] with PAGA method. Specify [START_CELL_TYPE] as the root cell type and detect [N] lineage branches. Generate trajectory plots with branch probabilities.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user exploring complex branching structures in the data

prompt_templates.title: Full Analysis Pipeline with Custom Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute a complete pseudotime analysis on [DATA_PATH]. Use diffusion method with 50 principal components, 20 neighbors for graph construction, and 8 diffusion components. Set [CELL_TYPE] as the root cell type and detect up to 4 lineages. Plot expression of all highly variable genes. Generate PDF output at 600 DPI resolution.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user tuning every parameter for a publication-ready analysis

output_examples.input: Run pseudotime trajectory analysis on my data.h5ad file with the diffusion method.
output_examples.output: The skill produced a 4-panel trajectory plot showing cells colored by pseudotime value, cluster assignment, and lineage identity. A pseudotime distribution histogram was generated. Pseudotime values and lineage assignments were saved as CSV files. The analysis report JSON documents parameters, cell counts, and lineage statistics.

output_examples.input: Plot SOX2, NANOG, and OCT4 gene expression along the pseudotime trajectory for my stem cell data.
output_examples.output: The skill produced individual gene trend plots for each marker showing expression increasing or decreasing along pseudotime. A combined heatmap displayed all three genes with cells ordered by pseudotime. Trend lines fitted to the data reveal when each gene is activated during differentiation.

output_examples.input: I need to see the branching structure of my T-cell differentiation data.
output_examples.output: The skill produced a lineage tree visualization showing branching points in T-cell development. Branch probability estimates were calculated for each cell, identifying two main lineages corresponding to CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell paths. A PAGA connectivity graph highlighted cluster relationships.

best_practices: Validate computed trajectories against known marker gene expression patterns and biological priors to confirm the inferred differentiation path is biologically meaningful.
Run the analysis with multiple trajectory methods (diffusion and PAGA) on the same dataset and compare results before drawing biological conclusions.
Correct batch effects and regress out cell cycle-related genes before running trajectory inference to avoid confounding differentiation signals.
anti_patterns: Interpreting precise pseudotime values as absolute biological time. Pseudotime is a relative ordering, not a real time measurement.
Running trajectory analysis on datasets with strong batch effects or mixed samples from different experimental conditions without correction.
Using trajectory inference on datasets with insufficient cell type diversity, as the algorithm may create artificial branching structures.

faq.question: What input format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The skill requires a preprocessed AnnData file in .h5ad format. The input data should already be normalized, log-transformed, and should contain cluster annotations (default: leiden) and optionally cell type annotations.

faq.question: How does the skill determine the starting cell for trajectories?
faq.answer: The skill tries three methods in order: (1) a user-specified cell ID, (2) a user-specified cell type with highest stemness marker expression, or (3) auto-detection by finding the cell with highest expression of stemness markers like SOX2 and NANOG.

faq.question: What trajectory inference methods are available?
faq.answer: The skill supports diffusion pseudotime (DPT) using scanpy as the default method and PAGA (partition-based graph abstraction) as an alternative. Both methods generate pseudotime values, lineage assignments, and visualizations.

faq.question: Can I visualize specific gene expression patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. You can specify a comma-separated list of gene names with the --genes parameter. The skill generates individual trend plots for each gene along pseudotime and a combined heatmap showing all genes sorted by pseudotime.

faq.question: How does the skill handle large datasets?
faq.answer: Datasets with more than 50,000 cells may require 16 GB or more of RAM. The skill uses efficient scanpy data structures and subsampling is recommended for very large datasets if memory becomes an issue.

faq.question: What if the computed trajectory does not match my biological expectations?
faq.answer: First verify root cell selection is correct. Try specifying a different starting cell type or cell ID. Second, try the alternative PAGA method. If results still disagree with biology, the data may not have sufficient differentiation signal or may have strong batch effects that need correction.
